comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: TESTING STRATEGY: TUI tests are the hardest to write well. Use the existing test harness pattern (construct BvrApp with test data, send key events, assert state). For new ViewModes, the minimum viable test is: enter the mode, verify no panic, verify some expected data appears. For complex interactions (time-travel, modals), write multi-step keyflow sequences. The keyflow e2e script is critical for catching regressions — it should be fast (<10s) and cover all view mode transitions.

title: Implement differentiated exit codes for --check-drift (0=OK, 1=critical, 2=warning)
description: Go's --check-drift returns exit code 0 when no drift detected, 1 when critical drift found, 2 when only warnings. Rust's --robot-drift (robot_drift flag in cli.rs:306) runs drift analysis but doesn't use differentiated exit codes — it always exits 0 on success. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) In src/main.rs where robot_drift is handled, after computing drift results, check severity. (2) If any critical-severity drifts found, return ExitCode::from(1). (3) If only warning-severity, return ExitCode::from(2). (4) If clean, return ExitCode::SUCCESS. (5) Document exit codes in --robot-docs output. Go ref: main.go line 125 comment. ACCEPTANCE: Test that critical drift produces exit 1, warning-only produces exit 2, clean produces exit 0.
close_reason: Already implemented in analysis/drift.rs:381-387. compute_drift() returns exit_code: 1 for critical, 2 for warning, 0 for OK. Tests verify this at drift.rs:583 and drift.rs:600. No work needed.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: cmd/bv/main.go checkDrift() — returns exit code 0 for OK, 1 for critical drift detected, 2 for warnings only. Our Rust --check-drift always returns 0 or 1. Scripts use exit codes to gate CI/CD pipelines, so the three-level scheme is important.

IMPLEMENTATION: (1) In src/robot.rs encode_json(), check std::env::var('BV_PRETTY_JSON'). (2) If set to '1', use serde_json::to_string_pretty() instead of to_string(). (3) This should already be partially implemented — verify and add test. Go ref: main.go line 7485. ACCEPTANCE: BV_PRETTY_JSON=1 bvr --robot-triage produces indented JSON.

title: Convert Issue timestamp fields from String to DateTime<Utc> for type consistency
description: Rust's Issue struct stores created_at, updated_at, due_date, closed_at as Option<String> while Sprint stores start_date, end_date as Option<DateTime<Utc>>. This inconsistency means timestamp comparisons in analysis code require repeated parsing. Go uses time.Time for all timestamp fields. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Change created_at, updated_at, due_date, closed_at in src/model.rs from Option<String> to Option<DateTime<Utc>> using chrono. (2) Use #[serde(deserialize_with)] for flexible parsing (ISO8601/RFC3339). (3) Update all code that accesses these fields — grep for '.created_at', '.updated_at', '.due_date', '.closed_at' across src/. (4) Update serialization to maintain RFC3339 string output. (5) Comment.created_at should also be converted. WARNING: This is a wide-reaching change touching model.rs, triage.rs, diff.rs, suggest.rs, history.rs, robot.rs, main.rs, tui.rs. Thorough testing required. ACCEPTANCE: All timestamp fields are DateTime<Utc>. All 627+ tests still pass. JSON output unchanged.
close_reason: Converted Issue/Dependency/Comment timestamp fields from Option<String> to Option<DateTime<Utc>>. Removed 7 duplicate parse_timestamp functions across modules. All 779 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: REFACTOR: Currently Issue struct uses String for created_at, updated_at, closed_at. Go uses time.Time. This touches model.rs, loader.rs, and any code that reads/writes these fields. Test carefully — date parsing edge cases (missing timezone, epoch 0, future dates) must all work. BLOCKS bd-3dh.12 (time-travel needs real timestamps).

title: Separate is_closed() from is_tombstone() in status semantics
description: Go distinguishes IsClosed() (only 'closed') from IsTombstone() (only 'tombstone'), while Rust's is_closed_like() treats BOTH as closed. This affects issue counting: tombstoned issues shouldn't appear in 'closed' counts, they're permanently removed records. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) In src/model.rs, split is_closed_like() into is_closed() (status == 'closed'), is_tombstone() (status == 'tombstone'), and is_terminal() (either). (2) Update call sites: analysis/graph counting should use is_terminal() (neither open nor working). Triage counts should distinguish. Robot output counts (open/closed/tombstone) should be separate. (3) Keep is_open_like() as \!is_terminal() for backward compat. Go ref: pkg/model/types.go Status.IsClosed(), Status.IsTombstone(). ACCEPTANCE: is_closed() returns false for tombstone. Separate counts in triage output.
close_reason: Added is_closed() and is_tombstone() methods to Issue model. is_closed_like() retained as terminal status check. SnapshotCounts in diff.rs now tracks tombstone separately with terminal() accessor. 1 new unit test. 474 lib tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/model/issue.go — is_closed() means work is done. is_tombstone() means the issue was deleted/merged/superseded. Currently Rust treats both the same (any non-open status). The distinction matters for metrics — tombstoned issues shouldn't count in velocity calculations but closed ones should.

title: Async Phase 2: background metric computation for large graphs
description: WHAT: For graphs above a configurable size threshold, compute expensive metrics (betweenness centrality, eigenvector centrality) in a background thread while returning fast metrics (PageRank, k-core, degree) immediately. WHY: Go handles this via goroutines in pkg/analysis/analysis.go — expensive metrics compute in background while the TUI shows partial results. Our Rust engine blocks on all metrics before returning anything, which makes large graphs feel sluggish. This is lower priority because most real-world beads graphs are small (<1000 nodes), but it's important for the 'wow factor' on large repos. IMPLEMENTATION: Use std::thread::spawn (not async — we don't have a runtime and don't need one for CPU-bound work). GraphAnalyzer returns AnalysisResult with Option<T> for expensive metrics. TUI polls for completion and re-renders when background metrics arrive. Config system (bd-6gv) provides the size threshold. ACCEPTANCE: Fast metrics return in <100ms regardless of graph size. Expensive metrics fill in within seconds. TUI shows progress indicator while background computation runs. No unsafe code. DEPENDS ON: bd-6gv (config system for thresholds). PARENT: bd-3in

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: DESIGN: Two-phase result: AnalysisResult{fast: FastMetrics, slow: Option<SlowMetrics>}. FastMetrics: PageRank, degree, k-core (all O(V+E) or near). SlowMetrics: betweenness (O(VE)), eigenvector (iterative). TUI renders fast immediately, shows spinner for slow, re-renders on completion via channel notification. Thread join on app exit to prevent resource leak.

The cache (bd-4h6) is needed by WhatIf (bd-ybj.4) for fast delta computation. The feedback loop (bd-3ud) is needed by triage output (bd-1ru.9). title: Optimize robot-triage startup hotspots with measurement-backed changes
description:
Background:
Robot-oriented triage and planning flows are usable, but startup still spends measurable time in analyzer construction and triage computation on medium stress fixtures.

Goal:
Use measurement-backed optimization passes to reduce robot-command startup latency without changing public APIs or output semantics.
design:
Implementation expectations:
1. Use rch for all cargo-based measurement and verification commands.
2. Profile first, then change one internal lever at a time.
3. Keep or revert candidate optimizations strictly from measured evidence.
4. Preserve output equivalence for triage, plan, and priority flows and record the benchmark and profile commands used.
acceptance_criteria:
1. At least one startup or performance hotspot is improved with measured evidence.
2. Focused correctness checks pass after the final change set.
3. The bead notes summarize the commands used and the measured before and after data.
notes:
Created to track the current optimization pass requested by the user.

Completed sub-tasks:
1. Collected dense, sparse, and cyclic triage timings with criterion via rch.
2. Ran an A/B comparison for the triage lookup cache.
3. Kept the cache based on measured wins across dense, sparse, and cyclic fixtures.
4. Added a follow-up graph-metric allocation cut in compute_metrics_with_config to stop cloning blocker and dependent vectors just to read their lengths.

Measured cache A/B:
- dense/500 post-cache: [1.0317 ms 1.0591 ms 1.0781 ms]
- dense/500 baseline: [1.2785 ms 1.2933 ms 1.3093 ms]
- sparse/500 post-cache: [1.2719 ms 1.3588 ms 1.4169 ms]
- sparse/500 baseline: [1.4358 ms 1.5394 ms 1.6573 ms]
- cyclic/500 post-cache: [994.26 us 1.0147 ms 1.0431 ms]
- cyclic/500 baseline: [1.0452 ms 1.1018 ms 1.1406 ms]

Measured follow-up graph-metric cut:
- stress_fixture/analyzer_new_triage_runtime before: [2.1655 ms 2.2400 ms 2.3670 ms]
- stress_fixture/analyzer_new_triage_runtime after: [2.0976 ms 2.1893 ms 2.2583 ms]
- startup profile after the follow-up cut: load_jsonl=0.922ms, build_graph=2.5ms, triage=1.5ms, insights=0.680ms, total=5.6ms

Residual blockers:
1. Agent Mail MCP transport is intermittent; CLI registration and file reservations worked, but resource reads and message operations were unreliable.
2. `cargo test --lib triage_runtime` on the current tree hit unrelated test-target errors in src/analysis/alerts.rs (`parse_timestamp` not found).
3. One `rch` bench attempt for analyzer_new_full fell back to local execution after rsync trouble, so it was not used as evidence.

title: Robot output unit tests + e2e validation scripts
description: WHAT: Comprehensive test coverage for all robot output changes in this epic. THREE layers: (1) Unit tests for each new field/struct — verify serialization shape, field presence, correct types, default values. (2) Conformance tests comparing Rust JSON output against Go reference fixtures for every robot mode (--robot-triage, --robot-plan, --robot-insights, --robot-next, --robot-priority). (3) E2e bash script (scripts/test_robot_output_parity.sh) that runs both bv and bvr against a shared test fixture, diffs the JSON schemas field-by-field, and logs discrepancies with context. LOGGING: Each test should print what it's checking, expected vs actual, and on failure dump the full JSON for debugging. E2e script should use set -euo pipefail, trap for cleanup, and produce a summary report at the end. ACCEPTANCE: All robot outputs have matching JSON schemas. Zero field-name or type mismatches. E2e script passes with exit 0. Conformance fixtures updated. PARENT: bd-1ru

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: TESTING STRATEGY: This bead covers ALL test artifacts for Epic 1 (Robot Output). Tests should be written incrementally — add unit tests for each field as it's implemented, then the conformance fixtures once all fields exist, then the e2e script as the capstone. The e2e script should be runnable standalone (scripts/test_robot_output_parity.sh) and also integrated into CI. Key insight: Go may not have ALL the fields we're adding (some may be aspirational) — the conformance tests should compare against Go where Go has the field, and validate presence/type where it doesn't. Use insta snapshot tests for JSON shape validation.

title: Infrastructure unit tests + integration tests
description: WHAT: Test coverage for config, cache, feedback, and async. (1) Config tests: AnalysisConfig loads from YAML file, from env var, from CLI flag. Merge with defaults works (missing fields get defaults). Invalid config rejected with clear error. Disabled metrics actually skipped. Timeout enforcement works (mock slow computation). Size thresholds respected. (2) Cache tests: cache hit returns same result. Cache miss computes fresh. TTL expiry works (fast-forward time). Graph change invalidates cache (hash differs). Thread-safe concurrent access. Memory cap enforced. (3) Feedback tests: feedback.json round-trips correctly. Score deltas apply in correct direction. Missing file = no feedback (not error). Corrupt file = clear error message. (4) Async tests: fast metrics return immediately. Slow metrics arrive eventually (use channel). TUI poll picks up completed results. Graph size below threshold runs synchronously. (5) E2e script (scripts/test_infra_parity.sh): end-to-end config → cache → feedback → triage pipeline with verbose logging at each stage. LOGGING: Every test logs config state, cache state (hits/misses/evictions), feedback state, timing info. ACCEPTANCE: All infrastructure modules have >80% coverage. E2e pipeline script passes. PARENT: bd-3in

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: TESTING STRATEGY: Infrastructure tests need both unit isolation AND integration. Unit: test each component in isolation with mocks. Integration: test the full pipeline (config → cache → analysis → feedback → triage). The cache TTL test needs a way to fast-forward time — either make the clock injectable or use a short TTL (100ms) and sleep. The async test needs a way to control when 'slow' computation completes — use a channel/barrier pattern.

title: Feedback loop: persistent scoring adjustments
description: WHAT: Implement a feedback mechanism where users can adjust triage scores (e.g. 'this issue is more important than the algorithm thinks') and those adjustments persist across sessions. WHY: Go has adjustScore() in pkg/analysis/triage.go that reads from .beads/feedback.json to apply user overrides to computed triage scores. This is the 'human-in-the-loop' piece that makes the triage engine actually useful over time — pure algorithmic scoring misses domain context. IMPLEMENTATION: Create src/analysis/feedback.rs. FeedbackStore struct loads/saves .beads/feedback.json (or BV_FEEDBACK_FILE env var). Each entry: {issue_id, score_delta, reason, timestamp}. Wire into triage scoring: after computing algorithmic score, apply any feedback deltas. Expose via --robot-feedback CLI flag for reading current feedback, and a TUI keybinding (f) for inline adjustment. ACCEPTANCE: Feedback persists in JSON file. Score adjustments apply on next triage run. --robot-feedback outputs current feedback state. TUI shows adjusted scores with visual indicator. DEPENDS ON: bd-ybj.2 (TriageScoringOptions — need the scoring infrastructure to hook into). PARENT: bd-3in
close_reason: Wired feedback weight adjustments into triage scoring pipeline. FeedbackData.weight_adjustment_map() produces HashMap for TriageScoringOptions.weight_adjustments. compute_impact_score() applies multipliers with renormalization. All 7 main.rs triage sites updated. 3 unit tests added (shift, empty baseline, renormalize). All 713 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: DESIGN: feedback.json format: [{issue_id: 'bd-xxx', delta: 0.3, reason: 'domain expert priority', timestamp: '2026-...', author: 'user@host'}]. Load on triage startup, apply deltas after algorithmic scoring. TUI 'f' key opens inline editor: up/down to adjust delta, Enter to confirm, text field for reason. DEPENDS ON bd-ybj.2 for the scoring hook point.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CORRECTION: Previous description referenced 'adjustScore()' which doesn't exist. The actual Go function is RecordFeedback() in feedback.go line 125, signature: func (f *FeedbackData) RecordFeedback(issueID, action string, score float64, breakdown ScoreBreakdown) error. Actions are 'accept' or 'ignore'. Weight adjustments use exponential smoothing with multipliers in 0.5-2.0 range. Feedback events are stored as FeedbackEvent structs with issue_id, action, score, timestamp. The weights adjusted are for all 8 ScoreBreakdown components: PageRank, Betweenness, BlockerRatio, Staleness, PriorityBoost, TimeToImpact, Urgency, Risk.

title: Cache layer: hash-based invalidation with TTL for graph metrics
description: WHAT: Add a computation cache that stores previously computed graph metrics keyed by a hash of the input graph structure, with configurable TTL expiry. WHY: Go caches analysis results (pkg/analysis/cache.go) so repeated triage/plan/insights calls on unchanged data are near-instant. Our Rust engine recomputes everything from scratch every time. For interactive TUI use, this causes noticeable lag when switching between views. IMPLEMENTATION: Create src/analysis/cache.rs with GraphMetricsCache struct. Key = SHA-256 of sorted (node_ids + edge_pairs). Value = all computed metrics (PageRank scores, betweenness, HITS, etc.). TTL configurable via AnalysisConfig (default 5 minutes). Cache is in-memory only (no disk persistence needed). Wire into GraphAnalyzer so compute_all_metrics checks cache first. ACCEPTANCE: Second call with same graph returns cached results in <1ms. Cache invalidates when graph changes. TTL expiry works. Cache hit/miss stats available for debugging. PARENT: bd-3in
close_reason: Created analysis/cache.rs with MetricsCache: SHA-256 keyed in-memory cache with configurable TTL (default 5 min), automatic expired-entry eviction, hit/miss stats. 9 unit tests (hit, miss, status-change invalidation, TTL expiry, clear, deterministic key, config-sensitive key, empty graph, default impl). All 725 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: DESIGN: Use a simple HashMap<[u8; 32], CachedMetrics> where the key is SHA-256 of the graph structure. CachedMetrics holds all computed values plus a timestamp. On cache check: hash current graph, look up, check TTL. Thread-safe via Arc<RwLock<>>. No disk persistence — cache is session-scoped. Memory bound: cap at ~100 entries (each is small, just numbers).

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CORRECTION: Previous description said 'No disk persistence needed.' However, Go cache.go has BOTH in-memory and disk caching (robotAnalysisDiskCacheFileName = 'analysis_cache.json'). For full parity we should implement disk caching too, at minimum for robot outputs. Memory cache uses sync.RWMutex (Rust: Arc<RwLock<>>). Disk cache writes to .beads/analysis_cache.json. Also noted: Go uses a global package-level cache instance (globalCache) — Rust should use a similar static or passed-through cache reference.

title: Config system: per-metric toggles, timeouts, size-based thresholds
description: WHAT: Implement AnalysisConfig struct with per-metric enable/disable toggles (e.g. enable_pagerank, enable_betweenness, enable_hits, enable_eigenvector, enable_kcore), computation timeouts per metric, and size-based thresholds (e.g. skip betweenness for graphs >10k nodes). WHY: Go's analysis engine has configurable analysis via AnalysisOptions struct (pkg/analysis/analysis.go) that lets users tune which metrics run and their resource bounds. Our Rust engine currently hardcodes all metrics as always-on with no timeouts. Without this, large graphs can hang or OOM. IMPLEMENTATION: Add AnalysisConfig to src/analysis/graph.rs, wire it through GraphAnalyzer::new(), expose via --analysis-config CLI flag (YAML/JSON file) and BV_ANALYSIS_CONFIG env var. Default config should match current behavior (all enabled, generous timeouts). ACCEPTANCE: Config file loads, metrics can be individually disabled, timeouts abort long-running computations gracefully, robot output includes which metrics were skipped and why. PARENT: bd-3in
close_reason: Implemented AnalysisConfig with per-metric enable/disable toggles and size-based thresholds. Added SkippedMetric tracking, compute_metrics_with_config(), factory methods (full/for_size/triage_only), serde support, and Analyzer::new_with_config(). 9 new unit tests. All 669 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: DESIGN: AnalysisConfig should be a simple YAML/JSON file: {metrics: {pagerank: {enabled: true, max_iterations: 100}, betweenness: {enabled: true, timeout_ms: 5000, max_nodes: 10000}, ...}, cache: {ttl_seconds: 300}, async: {background_threshold: 1000}}. Load from file path, env var, or CLI flag. Merge with defaults (all enabled, generous limits). Serialize back to JSON for robot output echo.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: ADDITIONAL DETAIL from Go config.go: The AnalysisConfig has per-metric fields: ComputeBetweenness (with BetweennessMode: exact/approximate/skip), ComputePageRank, ComputeHITS, ComputeCycles, ComputeEigenvector, ComputeCriticalPath, ComputeKCore, ComputeArticulation, ComputeSlack. Each has associated timeout fields and skip reasons. Go provides factory functions: DefaultConfig() (all enabled, exact betweenness), ConfigForSize(nodeCount, edgeCount int) (adaptive), FullAnalysisConfig(), TriageConfig() (PageRank+Betweenness only), NoPhase2Config(). Env overrides: BV_SKIP_PHASE2, BV_PHASE2_TIMEOUT_S via ApplyEnvOverrides(). Rust should implement all factory functions.

title: Close remaining legacy parity gaps vs beads_viewer Go
description:
Background:
The Rust port already covers most robot-mode and baseline TUI behavior, but the parity audit found that full legacy replacement is still blocked by a concentrated set of gaps: static export/pages fidelity, preview/watch/pages UX, workspace default-discovery behavior, richer history search modes, and several high-touch TUI interaction surfaces. This epic is now the canonical source of truth for finishing that work.

Goal:
Finish the remaining legacy parity work in a way that is implementation-ready for future agents without requiring them to reread the historical markdown planning docs or reconstruct intent from old sessions.

Why this matters:
The project goal is not just partial feature coverage; it is a trustworthy Rust replacement for the legacy Go `bv` binary. Until the remaining surfaces below are closed and re-verified, the project cannot honestly claim complete functional parity.
design:
Execution model: Treat this bead as the umbrella parity epic and planning container. Future agents should usually claim leaf tasks, not this container, unless they are actively curating the plan itself. Keep the remaining work split into self-contained feature tracks: export or artifacts, preview/watch/pages UX, workspace/history parity, TUI parity, and docs or architecture alignment. Require an explicit verification ladder for every completed track: fast unit or state tests for pure logic, integration or conformance tests for IO contracts, shell-level e2e scripts for operator journeys, and a final `rch`-routed sweep. Use explicit dependencies to encode both technical sequencing and practical merge-risk constraints when multiple tasks touch the same Rust modules. Every implementation bead must carry its own validation burden; do not defer evidence gathering to a final cleanup pass.

Validation policy: All cargo builds, clippy runs, and test runs must continue to go through `rch`. E2E scripts and parity harnesses must preserve detailed step logs, artifact paths, and focused failure summaries so regressions are diagnosable without ad hoc reproduction. Update parity docs only after the underlying behavior and all required evidence layers are green.
acceptance_criteria:
- All child feature tracks and their leaf tasks are completed.
- `FEATURE_PARITY.md` and `README.md` reflect reality instead of stale claims.
- Export/pages, workspace/history, and TUI parity gaps called out in the audit are either implemented or explicitly de-scoped with written rationale.
- Each user-facing track is backed by unit, integration, and shell-e2e evidence with detailed failure logging.
- Final parity verification passes through `rch` with clean `cargo check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and the relevant lib, integration, conformance, and e2e suites.
notes:
Current audit snapshot to preserve for future agents: Rust now has baseline pages export, export hooks for markdown and pages, workspace-aware watch polling, preview status endpoint support, and browser-open support. Remaining major export/pages gap: legacy Go still ships a richer SQLite-backed static export with bundled viewer assets, offline/CSP behavior, deployment artifacts, and a real interactive pages wizard. Remaining workspace/history gap: explicit workspace config support exists, but Go-style workspace discovery/default behavior and history search modes are still behind. Remaining TUI gap: board, insights, graph, and main-detail surfaces are functional but still less rich than the legacy Go UI. Documentation gap: some old parity rows overclaimed implemented behavior or lumped already-ported work into stale `planned` buckets.

Planning rule: The feature and epic beads in this subtree are coordination containers. Execution priority should usually live on their leaf tasks so `bv` recommendations stay actionable for implementers. Keep these container beads low-priority and normally `open`, not `in_progress`, unless someone is actively curating the plan itself.

title: Complete static export artifact parity with legacy bv
description:
Background:
The current Rust `--export-pages` surface is a baseline JSON/HTML bundle. The legacy Go export is materially richer: SQLite-backed data model, bundled viewer assets, offline/static-host readiness, and additional generated artifacts used by GitHub Pages and other deployment flows.

Goal:
Make Rust static export a functional replacement for the legacy export artifact contract instead of a reduced baseline.
design:
Approach:
- Freeze the artifact contract from legacy tests first so implementation is driven by executable expectations instead of memory.
- Land backend/export semantics before polishing wizard/deploy UX.
- Keep this feature focused on what `--export-pages` writes to disk; preview/watch/wizard behavior lives in a sibling feature track.
- Treat this track as complete only when it has the full verification ladder: reusable contract fixtures, code-level unit or integration evidence, and shell e2e proof with retained artifact logs.
- Prefer a discoverable operator workflow: parity scripts should plug into an existing e2e entrypoint or a clearly documented parity runner so future maintainers do not have to remember bespoke commands.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child tasks under this feature are completed.
- `--export-pages` writes the artifact set needed to stand in for the legacy Go export surface.
- The resulting behavior is backed by executable contract fixtures, integration or conformance tests, and shell e2e evidence with detailed logging rather than doc assertions alone.
- A future maintainer can rerun the export proof set from a documented entrypoint and understand failures from the retained logs and artifact inventory.
notes:
Audit context:
Legacy Go currently writes `beads.sqlite3`, `beads.sqlite3.config.json`, richer JSON payloads, viewer assets, and deployment-friendly extras. The Rust port currently emits a much smaller bundle and therefore still understates its remaining parity gap.

title: Freeze the canonical legacy export artifact contract in Rust tests
description:
Background:
Legacy export behavior is currently spread across many Go e2e tests. If future agents implement export parity from memory, they will keep missing edge cases and artifact details.

Goal:
Translate the legacy export artifact inventory and expectations into Rust-side tests or fixture assertions so the contract is local, executable, and self-documenting.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Mine the legacy export, offline, incremental, and cloudflare tests for concrete artifact expectations.
- Capture required files, metadata shapes, and behavioral invariants directly in Rust tests or compact fixture helpers.
- Use this task to settle naming and required/optional artifact categories before larger export refactors begin.
- Record provenance for each contract item so future agents can tell which legacy test or behavior it came from and why it matters.
- Classify each artifact or invariant as must-have parity, explicitly deferred, or non-goal, and make test failures name the category and missing contract item precisely.
- Shape the helpers so later integration tests and shell e2e scripts can reuse the same contract vocabulary instead of inventing parallel checklists.
acceptance_criteria:
- Rust test helpers describe the required export artifact inventory and major invariants.
- Future export tasks can point to Rust tests instead of rereading legacy Go test suites.
- The artifact contract clearly distinguishes must-have parity items from intentionally deferred or non-goal items.
- Contract fixtures or helpers preserve legacy provenance and produce precise failure messages that identify which artifact, invariant, or contract class regressed.
- Later export verification beads can reuse this contract directly rather than restating it in ad hoc assertions.
notes:
This task is deliberately front-loaded to avoid repeated debate about whether a missing file or payload is a true parity gap. It should encode the audit findings into testable local truth.

Future-self guidance: if a later export decision feels ambiguous, the answer should come from the contract produced here. If the contract seems incomplete, extend it with provenance and rationale before implementing more export behavior.
close_reason: Encoded legacy export contract in Rust tests with provenance/status matrix; validated via rch in isolated clean worktree (fmt/check/clippy + contract tests).

title: Port the SQLite-backed export backend and config payloads
description:
Background:
The largest remaining export gap is that legacy Go ships a SQLite-backed static viewer model, while Rust currently exports only plain JSON payloads.

Goal:
Implement the Rust-side export backend needed to emit `beads.sqlite3`, `beads.sqlite3.config.json`, and the core structured data expected by the richer viewer flows.
design:
Execution model:
This bead is now a planning container for the SQLite export backend. Child beads carry the implementation priority for schema/writer contract, data population, bootstrap config payloads, and compatibility handling for existing reduced JSON sidecars.

Integration rule:
Close this parent only when the child beads integrate into one deterministic `--export-pages` path and the export verification bead can exercise the resulting database and config artifacts without hidden TODOs.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child implementation beads under this container are completed.
- `--export-pages` emits the SQLite database and companion config JSON required by the legacy static viewer workflow.
- The exported database is non-empty, structurally valid, and ready for the export verification bead to test as one coherent backend.
- Existing reduced JSON outputs are either preserved or intentionally replaced with documented rationale rather than disappearing by accident.
notes: This bead was decomposed because it mixed schema design, writer behavior, config payloads, and compatibility decisions. Future agents should claim the child beads, not this container.

title: Define the exported SQLite schema and deterministic database writer contract
description:
Background:
The current SQLite export bead mixes schema design, database creation semantics, and higher-level payload concerns. That makes it too easy to argue about table shape while implementation and validation are still fuzzy.

Goal:
Establish the deterministic SQLite schema and creation path that the rest of the export backend will build on.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Translate the frozen export contract into a concrete SQLite schema, creation path, and deterministic writer contract.
- Be explicit about table or view names, bootstrap metadata placement, PRAGMAs, and any ordering or hashing rules that affect deterministic output.
- Add focused implementation-adjacent tests for schema creation and deterministic DB bootstrap behavior, even though the full proof set lands in the export verification bead.
acceptance_criteria: - Rust can create an empty but structurally valid export database that matches the agreed schema contract.\n- Schema-level invariants are explicit enough that later population/config beads do not have to infer them.\n- Focused tests fail clearly when the database skeleton or deterministic writer contract changes unexpectedly.
notes: This bead is about the database skeleton and creation semantics, not yet the full data population problem.
close_reason: Implemented export_sqlite schema/bootstrap module with deterministic database contract, FTS/materialized-view rebuild helpers, and focused schema tests; validated via rch (cargo check, cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --lib in clean worktree).

title: Populate the canonical export database with core issue, history, workspace, and summary data
description:
Background:
Once the export database skeleton exists, Rust still needs to populate the records and views that make the exported viewer actually useful.

Goal:
Map loaded Rust data into the canonical exported tables or views needed for the legacy-equivalent static viewer workflow.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Populate the canonical exported tables or views from the loaded Rust model, including the core issue, history, workspace, and summary data the viewer expects.
- Preserve deterministic row ordering or keying wherever it affects export diffs or downstream viewer behavior.
- Add focused tests or fixtures for the population logic so the later verification bead can focus on end-to-end conformance instead of every low-level mapping edge case.
acceptance_criteria: - The export database contains the core records and views needed for the viewer to answer its main queries.\n- Representative datasets produce non-empty, structurally consistent database contents.\n- Population regressions are caught by focused tests or fixtures before the full export conformance bead runs.
notes: This bead owns data population and mapping fidelity, not the separate config/bootstrap contract.
close_reason: Already complete: populate_export_database() maps all core data (issues, deps, comments, metrics, triage, meta) + rebuilds MV and FTS. 8 unit tests + 3 integration tests verify population correctness.

title: Emit and validate the viewer bootstrap config payloads for the SQLite export
description:
Background:
The database alone is not enough; the viewer also expects bootstrap config payloads that explain how to open and interpret the exported bundle.

Goal:
Produce the required companion config payloads and make their contract explicit and testable.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Emit the companion config payloads that tell the exported viewer how to open and interpret the SQLite bundle.
- Keep the payload shape deterministic, explicit, and aligned with the legacy contract frozen earlier.
- Add focused tests for serialization and required-field behavior so configuration drift is easy to spot.
acceptance_criteria:
- `beads.sqlite3.config.json` or equivalent bootstrap payloads are emitted with the required fields and structure.
- Config payloads remain synchronized with the exported database expectations.
- Focused tests fail clearly when required fields, naming, or bootstrap semantics drift.
notes: This bead closes the bootstrap/config half of the SQLite export contract and should stay separate from raw row population.
close_reason: Completed: emit deterministic SQLite bootstrap config payloads and focused tests

title: Preserve or intentionally replace the reduced JSON sidecars during the SQLite export transition
description:
Background:
The Rust exporter already emits reduced JSON artifacts. Moving to the richer SQLite backend must not accidentally break those consumers or leave the replacement story implicit.

Goal:
Make the compatibility or replacement decision explicit and implement it in the exporter so no capability disappears by accident.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Decide explicitly whether the reduced JSON sidecars stay, change shape, or are retired in favor of the richer SQLite backend.
- Preserve user-facing functionality unless a documented replacement is already in place.
- Add focused tests or assertions so compatibility behavior cannot silently disappear during the transition.
acceptance_criteria: - Existing reduced JSON outputs are either preserved or intentionally replaced with explicit rationale.\n- No already-working export consumer path disappears by accident during the SQLite migration.\n- Focused tests or assertions make the compatibility decision executable rather than purely textual.
notes: This bead exists specifically to prevent feature loss while the export backend becomes richer.
close_reason: Already complete: export_pages_bundle() emits all JSON sidecars (issues, meta, triage, insights, history, export_summary) alongside SQLite. Legacy export contract tests (observe_meta_json, observe_triage_json, observe_history_json) verify no sidecar path disappears. Coexistence is explicit in the code.

title: Port the viewer asset pack and offline/CSP export semantics
description:
Background:
Legacy Go export bundles a real viewer asset pack, including JS/CSS/vendor assets, browser-side scoring helpers, and offline/static-host details. The Rust export still uses a reduced bespoke HTML/CSS/JS bundle.

Goal:
Bring the exported asset pack and offline readiness closer to the legacy viewer so the static bundle behaves like a real deployment artifact rather than a debug export.
design:
Execution model:
This bead is now a planning container for asset-pack parity. Child beads carry the implementation priority for asset inventory packaging, offline/static-host reference behavior, and CSP/cache/runtime browser semantics.

Integration rule:
Close this parent only when the packaged assets and browser-facing semantics work together as one coherent exported viewer bundle.
acceptance_criteria: - Child implementation beads under this container are completed.\n- Exported bundles contain the asset set and reference semantics needed for legacy-equivalent static viewing workflows.\n- Offline/static-host behavior and CSP/cache/runtime expectations are explicit enough for the export verification bead to exercise them without guessing.
notes: This bead was decomposed because 'asset parity' actually hides several separable problems: packaging the files, referencing them correctly, and preserving the browser-runtime semantics around them.

title: Package the canonical viewer asset inventory deterministically
description:
Background:
The asset-pack bead currently bundles inventory decisions, packaging, and host behavior into one large step.

Goal:
Identify and package the full viewer asset inventory required for parity in a deterministic, testable way.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Identify the full asset inventory required for parity and package it deterministically into the export bundle.
- Make file naming, placement, and bundling rules explicit enough that later offline/runtime beads can assume a stable asset set.
- Add focused checks for asset presence and deterministic packaging where practical.
acceptance_criteria: - The exported bundle contains the agreed viewer asset inventory.\n- Packaging is deterministic enough for later conformance tests to compare inventories without ad hoc normalization.\n- Focused checks catch missing or misnamed assets early.
notes: This bead is about asset inventory and packaging, not yet the host/offline/runtime semantics layered on top.
close_reason: Implemented canonical viewer asset inventory: new viewer_assets module with 24 embedded assets (index.html, viewer.js, styles.css, graph.js, charts.js, vendor/* with D3, Alpine, Mermaid, Chart.js, sql-wasm, fonts, WASM graph analytics). Deterministic write_viewer_assets() with sorted manifest. 11 tests (inventory, determinism, completeness). Updated export_pages_bundle() to write canonical assets. Promoted 4 parity contract items (graph-runtime, search-loader, root stylesheet, root viewer script) from deferred/non-goal to must-have.

title: Port static-host and offline-safe asset reference semantics
description:
Background:
Having the right files on disk is not enough if the exported HTML and runtime still assume the wrong path, host, or CDN model.

Goal:
Make the exported asset references behave correctly for offline and generic static-host deployment flows.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Make HTML and runtime references work for offline usage and generic static hosts, not just the local dev flow.
- Eliminate implicit CDN assumptions and make pathing, routing, and relative reference behavior explicit.
- Add focused tests or fixtures for representative host/offline path semantics where practical.
acceptance_criteria: - Asset references work under offline and generic static-host assumptions.\n- No required viewer path still depends on an external CDN or a dev-only host layout.\n- Focused checks make host/reference regressions easy to isolate.
notes: This bead is specifically about host/offline semantics, which are easy to break even when all the right files exist.
close_reason: Added offline-safe asset reference verification: 3 new tests (no external URLs, all script/link refs resolve to inventory, CSP meta tag present). Updated parity contract: promoted offline-graph-runtime-pack, search-loader-assets, root-stylesheet, root-viewer-script from deferred/non-goal to must-have-now. Updated meta-title and local-bootstrap observers for canonical index.html semantics. 14 total viewer_assets tests pass.

title: Port CSP, cache, and browser-runtime semantics for the exported viewer
description:
Background:
The legacy bundle encoded browser-runtime assumptions such as CSP, caching, and worker or vendor runtime behavior that are easy to miss if asset parity is treated as a file-copy problem.

Goal:
Restore the browser-runtime semantics that make the exported viewer safe and functional in real deployments.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Restore the CSP, cache, and browser-runtime semantics that matter for the exported viewer to behave safely and predictably.
- Be explicit about worker, vendor, bootstrap, or script-loading behavior that affects deployed bundles.
- Add focused assertions for the runtime contract where deterministic checks are possible.
acceptance_criteria: - The exported viewer honors the agreed CSP/cache/runtime contract.\n- Browser-side bootstrap behavior remains compatible with the packaged asset model and static-host assumptions.\n- Focused assertions make CSP/runtime regressions visible before the full export parity suite runs.
notes: This bead prevents 'asset parity' from stopping at file counts while missing the actual browser-runtime contract.
close_reason: Added 10 focused CSP/cache/runtime assertions: CSP directive validation (7 directives), COI service worker presence+headers+registration, script load ordering, WASM runtime pairing, asset content types, preview server cache-control, exported bundle CSP/SW checks. All 789 tests pass.

title: Port auxiliary export artifacts such as project health metadata and deploy-facing docs
description:
Background:
Legacy Go export also emitted supporting artifacts that made the bundle usable in real deployment workflows: extra JSON like project health summaries, generated README content, and host-facing guidance or workflow material.

Goal:
Restore the deploy-facing and operations-facing artifacts that made legacy static export self-explanatory for GitHub Pages, Cloudflare Pages, and generic static hosts.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Identify which non-viewer artifacts are part of the real legacy export contract versus optional niceties.
- Implement the minimum set that materially affects usability, deployment parity, and self-description of the exported bundle.
- Avoid generating brittle boilerplate that is not validated anywhere.
acceptance_criteria:
- Rust export emits the agreed supplemental artifacts required by the contract.
- Integration tests verify their presence and key content or invariants.
- The bundle is more self-describing for future users and operators without depending on external docs.
notes: Examples from the audit include project-health-style data and generated README or deployment-facing outputs. The exact set should be locked by the export contract bead rather than guessed ad hoc.
close_reason: Implemented README.md and _headers generation in export bundle. Added STATIC_HOST_HEADERS constant, generate_deploy_readme() function, modified export_pages_bundle() to emit both files. 3 new lib tests, integration contract items promoted to MustHaveParityNow.

title: Build export unit, conformance, and integration coverage for full artifact parity
description:
Background:
Export parity claims are easy to overstate unless they are backed by executable evidence. This task exists to make the export feature track falsifiable and stable over time before the shell-e2e layer is added.

Goal:
Create or extend unit, integration, and conformance coverage so the Rust export surface can be verified against the artifact contract and key legacy behaviors.
design: Implementation expectations: Add module-level tests for deterministic export logic such as schema shaping, config serialization, artifact classification, and edge cases where those helpers can be exercised without full process launches. Add integration tests for artifact presence, SQLite or config validity, offline asset invariants, and generated supplemental outputs. The suite should explicitly cover a happy-path export, a representative minimal or edge-case export, and clear negative-path assertions for missing or malformed artifacts or schema invariants. Reuse or extend reference fixtures where that reduces ambiguity. Make failures precise enough that future agents can identify which aspect of export parity regressed, and shape the test outputs so they can feed the later shell-e2e bead cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: Export parity has explicit unit coverage for deterministic export logic and integration or conformance coverage for the full artifact matrix. The suite covers happy-path, representative edge-case, and negative regression scenarios, and fails when required artifacts or invariants are missing. Export parity can be validated with `rch` without manually inspecting the bundle.
notes: This bead is the code-level closure mechanism for the export artifact track. It intentionally stops short of shell-e2e workflow coverage, which is handled by the companion export/pages e2e bead, but it must already make failures and artifact mismatches easy to diagnose.
close_reason: Added 30 new unit tests for export_pages: empty issue list (2), title edge cases (4), meta JSON schema (1), triage/insights JSON shape (2), export summary (2), filtering (2), normalize path (4), MIME types (1), live reload (2), preview modes (3), idempotency (1), SQLite validation (2), headers contract (1), count/modified helpers (3). Also fixed graph_detail_text_for_width → graph_detail_text call sites and updated 3 graph snapshots. Total: 658 lib tests, 0 failures.

title: Add export/pages shell e2e parity script with diagnostic artifact capture
description:
Background:
Export parity still needs shell-level proof that the generated bundle works like a real operator artifact, not just that internal tests pass.

Goal:
Add a dedicated export/pages e2e script that exercises representative export flows and leaves behind enough diagnostics to debug failures quickly.
design: Implementation expectations: Create or extend a shell-level parity runner for export/pages flows, preferably as a dedicated script such as `scripts/e2e_parity_export.sh` if no better existing entrypoint already fits. Exercise representative success scenarios such as a normal export, a workspace-scoped export, and offline or static-host validation, plus at least one intentional failure-path scenario that proves the diagnostics are useful. Run the script in strict shell mode with timestamped step banners, echoed commands, environment and fixture summaries, temp-output paths, artifact inventory summaries, SQLite schema or table summaries, config JSON summaries, and preserved failing bundles or logs. Keep the script runnable both locally and under `rch`, with deterministic exit codes and failure text. Wire it into the existing e2e entrypoint if that remains the clearest operator workflow, or document a single parity-runner entrypoint that invokes it predictably alongside the other parity scripts.
acceptance_criteria:
- The export/pages e2e script proves the primary success path and at least one representative failure path for the export workflow.
- It exits nonzero when required artifacts are missing, the SQLite payload is invalid, or HTML and asset references violate the parity contract.
- Failure logs make it obvious which artifact or invariant broke, what command or fixture triggered it, and where the generated outputs were kept.
- The script is reachable from a documented e2e entrypoint or parity-runner workflow.
- This script becomes required evidence before export-track docs or the final parity sweep can claim completion.
notes: This bead is the shell-level proof that exported bundles are usable by operators and static-host users, not just structurally plausible to unit tests. Its logs should be good enough that a future agent can debug a failure from the retained artifacts alone.

title: Add export/pages shell e2e parity script with artifact inventory logging
description:
Background:
Users do not experience export parity through unit or integration tests; they experience it through a full export workflow that either yields a trustworthy static bundle or leaves them guessing. This bead makes that workflow observable end to end.

Goal:
Add a shell-e2e parity script for export and pages workflows with detailed artifact logging so export regressions are fast to diagnose.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Add or extend a shell-e2e entrypoint that exercises representative export or pages flows against stable fixture inputs.
- Emit timestamped step banners, echoed commands, bundle output paths, artifact inventories, SQLite or config summaries, and diff-friendly failure output.
- Preserve enough tempdir or log-path information on failure that future agents can diagnose missing assets, schema mismatches, or generated-doc regressions under `rch` without rerunning blindly.
- Keep the script non-interactive and deterministic enough for remote execution.
acceptance_criteria:
- A single shell-e2e entrypoint can validate export or pages workflows end to end under `rch`.
- Logs make it obvious which stage failed and what artifacts were actually produced.
- The script is referenced by the final verification bead and by final parity docs as part of the evidence chain.
notes: This bead is intentionally separate from the code-level export test bead so the project retains both fine-grained Rust-side assertions and operator-facing workflow evidence.
close_reason: Superseded by bd-7oo.1.6; duplicate export e2e planning bead created during concurrent refinement

title: Complete preview, watch, and pages UX parity with legacy bv
description:
Background:
Rust now has baseline preview serving, workspace-aware watch polling, a status endpoint, and browser-open support, but the legacy Go tool still provides a richer preview/watch/pages UX: fuller server lifecycle behavior, richer live reload semantics, more polished watch flows, and an interactive pages wizard.

Goal:
Close the remaining user-facing parity gap for previewing exported bundles, watching for changes, and walking users through pages deployment flows.
design:
Approach:
- Treat preview server semantics, watch-export behavior, and pages wizard flow as one cohesive UX track.
- Serialize tasks that touch `src/export_pages.rs`, `src/main.rs`, and the pages tests to reduce merge conflicts.
- Test actual user-visible behavior rather than just internal helper functions.
- Require the full verification ladder for this UX track: deterministic unit or integration coverage first, then shell e2e journeys with lifecycle traces, retained logs, and operator-facing diagnostics.
- Keep the final proof runnable from a discoverable entrypoint so preview/watch/pages regressions are easy to reproduce on demand.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child tasks under this feature are completed.
- Preview/watch/pages behavior is no longer merely a baseline substitute for the legacy flow.
- Tests cover the user-visible semantics that the audit identified as missing or simplified.
- This track has code-level and shell-level verification evidence with enough logging that startup, watch, wizard, and shutdown regressions are diagnosable without ad hoc reinstrumentation.
notes:
Current state:
The recent parity slice improved preview status and hook/watch plumbing, but did not yet port fsnotify/SSE live reload, graceful shutdown semantics, or the interactive wizard.

title: Port legacy preview-server semantics, including graceful lifecycle behavior
description:
Background:
The Rust preview server is serviceable, but it still differs from the legacy Go server in how it handles lifecycle, live reload, and full preview UX expectations.

Goal:
Bring the preview server behavior closer to the legacy contract so `--preview-pages` feels like the real product surface rather than a reduced helper.
design: Implementation expectations: Evaluate whether the current manual TCP loop should evolve toward a more structured HTTP server implementation so graceful shutdown, richer endpoints, and deterministic tests remain clean. Preserve the recently added status endpoint plus browser-open and no-browser escape hatches while extending lifecycle fidelity. Add user-facing diagnostics for startup URL, chosen port, port-binding failures, reload transport mode, and shutdown reason so preview failures are actionable instead of mysterious. If full legacy SSE or fsnotify is chosen here rather than in a separate bead, document the implementation boundary clearly.
acceptance_criteria: Preview server behavior matches the agreed lifecycle contract for startup, serving, stopping, and status reporting. The implementation preserves the existing status endpoint and browser or no-browser controls while extending parity. Operator-visible output makes port conflicts, current preview URL, reload behavior, and shutdown semantics easy to understand, including what the user should do next when startup fails. Any intentional deviations from legacy are explicit and justified in notes or docs.
notes: Implemented lifecycle parity on the current poll-based reload transport. Graceful shutdown, richer startup diagnostics, status/reporting parity, and actionable bind-failure guidance land here; fsnotify+SSE reload remains intentionally deferred because this bead focuses on server lifecycle semantics rather than changing the client reload transport.
close_reason: Ported preview lifecycle parity: graceful signal shutdown, richer status JSON, startup/shutdown diagnostics, actionable bind-failure guidance, and preview integration coverage; validated via rch (cargo fmt --check, cargo test --test export_pages preview_pages, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings in clean worktree).

title: Complete watch-export parity for workspace, change detection, and operator feedback
description:
Background:
Rust now watches the correct workspace source set, but the legacy Go watch flow still had richer operator-facing semantics such as clearer messaging, debounce behavior, and tighter pairing with preview flows.

Goal:
Make `--watch-export` behave like a reliable operator workflow rather than a minimal polling loop.
design: Implementation expectations: Review the remaining delta between current Rust watch polling and the legacy Go watch experience. Decide which semantics materially affect parity: debounce, logging, watched-path reporting, workspace behavior, preview guidance, no-op rebuild suppression, last-good-output behavior, and bounded verbosity. Make regeneration reasons and watched-source reporting visible enough that users can understand why the tool rebuilt or why it deliberately did not. Keep the design testable and avoid hidden timing assumptions where possible.
acceptance_criteria: Watch mode covers the agreed behavior for multi-source change detection, user messaging, and regeneration semantics. User-facing output explains which paths are being watched, why a rebuild was triggered or skipped, and whether the most recent cycle ended as a no-op, a successful rebuild, or a failed rebuild. Failure behavior is explicit enough that users can tell whether the last good export is still being served and what they should inspect next. Integration tests can demonstrate the intended behavior without manual inspection.
notes: This bead follows the preview-server work because both surfaces share files and user messaging. The goal is to reduce regressions and merge conflicts on the same code paths.

title: Port the interactive `--pages` wizard flow from legacy bv
description:
Background:
Legacy Go exposes `--pages` as a genuine interactive export, preview, and deploy wizard. Rust currently prints a non-interactive recommendation flow instead.

Goal:
Restore a real interactive pages workflow so users can discover and execute export or deploy steps without hand-assembling commands.
design:
Execution model:
This bead is now a planning container for the interactive `--pages` wizard. Child beads carry the implementation priority for the wizard state model, the substantive export/preview/deploy/review steps, and the user-facing boundary/diagnostic surfaces.

Integration rule:
Close this parent only when the children compose into one guided `--pages` journey rather than a set of disconnected screens or prompts.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child implementation beads under this container are completed.
- `--pages` is an interactive flow rather than a static help printer.
- The wizard covers the essential legacy journey and makes defaults, validation, review, file/action previews, and automation boundaries explicit enough for the verification beads to exercise deterministically.
notes: This bead was decomposed because the wizard work combines state-machine design, journey wiring, and user-safety/diagnostic concerns that are easier to implement and review as separate checkpoints.

title: Define the `--pages` wizard state model, defaults, validation, and cancellation semantics
description:
Background:
The pages wizard bead still bundles state-machine design, step content, and user-facing handoff semantics into one large implementation bucket.

Goal:
Lock down the wizard states, safe defaults, validation rules, and cancel or back behavior before the full journey is wired up.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Define the wizard state machine, default values, validation rules, and cancel/back semantics before wiring every step.
- Keep the model deterministic enough for debug-render and keyflow testing.
- Ensure the state machine preserves safe defaults and never leaves users unsure whether an action was committed.
acceptance_criteria: - Wizard states, transitions, defaults, and validation rules are explicit and testable.\n- Back/cancel behavior is safe and predictable.\n- Focused state tests or deterministic render coverage exist for the state model.
notes: This bead is the structural foundation for the pages wizard and should make the later step-implementation work much safer.

title: Implement the export, preview, deploy-target, and review steps of the `--pages` wizard
description:
Background:
Once the wizard states are stable, the main user journey still needs to connect export, preview, deploy-target selection, and review into one coherent flow.

Goal:
Implement the substantive wizard steps that make `--pages` a real guided workflow instead of a help screen.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Implement the actual export, preview, deploy-target, and review steps on top of the stable wizard state model.
- Reuse stable export/preview surfaces rather than duplicating their logic inside the wizard.
- Keep the resulting journey coherent enough that later verification can exercise it as a real user flow.
acceptance_criteria: - The wizard walks users through export, preview, deploy-target selection, and review as one connected journey.\n- Step content reflects the final export artifact and preview contract rather than placeholder text.\n- The implemented journey is stable enough for the later verification beads to test deterministically.
notes: This bead owns the substance of the wizard journey, not the separate question of how clearly it explains automation boundaries and file effects.

title: Make wizard automation boundaries, file previews, and debug transcripts explicit
description:
Background:
A good wizard is not only interactive; it also tells users exactly what it will do, what it will not do, and how to debug failures.

Goal:
Surface file or action previews, handoff boundaries, and transcript-friendly diagnostics so the pages wizard is safe and understandable.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Make automation boundaries, handoff points, file/action previews, and debug-friendly transcripts explicit in the wizard UX.
- Ensure users can tell what the wizard will do automatically, what they must do manually, and what outputs or side effects are about to be produced.
- Preserve enough transcript/debug information that later e2e logging can explain wizard failures cleanly.
acceptance_criteria: - The wizard previews the relevant files, actions, or outputs before committing work.\n- Automation boundaries and manual handoff points are obvious to the user.\n- Transcript/debug capture is explicit enough that later e2e logs can explain what the wizard attempted.
notes: This bead is the user-safety and diagnosability layer for the wizard, and it exists to prevent a superficially interactive flow from still being opaque.

title: Add preview, watch, and pages unit plus integration coverage
description:
Background:
Preview, watch, and pages regressions are hard to spot by code inspection alone because they involve timing, process lifecycle, wizard state, and human-facing messaging. This bead owns the code-level verification before the shell-e2e workflow layer is added.

Goal:
Create a reliable unit plus integration test suite for the full preview, watch, and pages track so parity claims are durable.
design: Implementation expectations: Add unit tests for deterministic lifecycle helpers, debounce or change-classification logic, prompt or message formatting, and wizard state transitions where those pieces can be isolated. Extend integration tests to cover preview status, bounded live-reload behavior, watch semantics, port-conflict handling, wizard entry points, validation failures, cancellation paths, and shutdown or lifecycle behavior as appropriate. Prefer deterministic request or response plus bounded-process techniques that work cleanly under `rch`. Keep failures focused on observable behavior rather than private implementation details, and make the outputs useful for the later shell-e2e bead.
acceptance_criteria: The preview, watch, and pages track has explicit unit coverage for deterministic logic and green integration coverage for its major user-visible behaviors. The suite covers happy-path flows plus representative edge and failure cases such as port conflicts, skipped rebuilds, failed rebuilds, invalid wizard input, and cancellation or back-out behavior. Test failures isolate whether the regression is in preview serving, change watching, or wizard flow. This bead is green before the shell-e2e workflow bead or parity docs claim the track is complete.
notes: This bead is the code-level closure gate for the preview, watch, and pages track. Rich shell-e2e journey coverage lives in the companion e2e bead so the two concerns stay distinct.

title: Add preview/watch/pages shell e2e script with lifecycle and watch trace logging
description:
Background:
Preview, watch, and pages behavior can pass narrow integration tests while still failing the real operator journey because of lifecycle timing, logging, or orchestration mistakes.

Goal:
Add a shell e2e script that exercises the preview/watch/pages journey end to end and records enough lifecycle detail to make failures obvious.
design: Implementation expectations: Create or extend a shell-level parity runner for `--preview-pages`, `--watch-export`, and the interactive `--pages` flow, preferably as a dedicated script such as `scripts/e2e_parity_preview_pages.sh` if that matches the repo better than extending an existing harness. Capture process logs, chosen port, request traces, touched files, rebuild reasons, debounce decisions, wizard transcript or debug capture, environment and fixture summaries, and shutdown events. Exercise the primary success path plus at least one failure-oriented scenario such as a preview bind failure or a broken rebuild input. Preserve the temporary workspace and emitted logs on failure. Depend on the export e2e bead so preview/pages verification runs against the final export bundle instead of a reduced intermediate artifact set. Wire this script into the main e2e entrypoint or a documented parity-runner so the operator does not need to remember a separate hidden command path.
acceptance_criteria:
- The preview/watch/pages e2e script proves startup, serving, rebuild, and shutdown behavior, plus the wizard journey where applicable, and it includes at least one representative failure-path run with retained diagnostics.
- Failure output isolates whether the regression is in preview serving, watch orchestration, wizard state handling, or export preparation.
- The script is reachable from a documented e2e entrypoint or parity-runner workflow.
- This evidence is required before docs or the final parity sweep can claim this track is complete.
notes: This bead captures the real operator journey for the pages surface. It exists because lifecycle-heavy flows often need richer transcripts than unit or integration tests provide.

title: Add preview/watch/pages shell e2e script with lifecycle and watch-trace logging
description:
Background:
Preview, watch, and pages are multi-process user journeys. Without a real shell-e2e workflow and detailed logs, regressions in timing, ports, watched paths, or wizard sequencing will remain painful to diagnose.

Goal:
Add a shell-e2e parity script for preview, watch, and pages workflows with detailed lifecycle and watch-trace logging.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Add or extend a shell-e2e entrypoint that exercises export-to-preview, watch-triggered regeneration, and pages-wizard journey coverage using bounded timeouts that work under `rch`.
- Emit timestamped step banners, echoed commands, chosen port and output path information, watched-path inventories, rebuild reasons, HTTP status traces, and wizard-step transcripts or summaries.
- Preserve enough tempdir or log-path information on failure that future agents can tell whether the problem was export preparation, preview lifecycle, change detection, or wizard orchestration.
- Keep the script deterministic and non-interactive, using scripted input where the wizard must be driven.
acceptance_criteria:
- A shell-e2e entrypoint validates the preview, watch, and pages workflows end to end under `rch`.
- Logs make it obvious whether failures occurred during export preparation, preview serving, change watching, or wizard progression.
- The script is referenced by the final verification bead and final parity docs as required evidence for this track.
notes: This bead deliberately depends on the export code-level verification bead as well as the preview-track verification bead so end-to-end pages workflows run against a validated export surface instead of an unstable intermediate state.
close_reason: Superseded by bd-7oo.2.5; duplicate preview/watch/pages e2e planning bead created during concurrent refinement

title: Close remaining workspace and history parity gaps
description:
Background:
The audit found that docs were stale here: Rust already has meaningful hooks, workspace aggregation, and history support. But two real gaps remain: Go-style workspace discovery/default behavior and richer history search modes in the TUI.

Goal:
Finish the genuinely missing workspace/history behavior and stop treating this area as an undifferentiated future bucket.
design:
Approach:
- Keep workspace default/discovery behavior separate from history UI search work so the tasks stay coherent.
- Add focused tests after each behavior lands to avoid reintroducing vague parity claims.
- Preserve the parts Rust already does well; this track is for the remaining deltas, not a rewrite.
- Require the full verification ladder here too: unit or loader or keyflow tests for semantics, then shell e2e runs with discovery and search logging that explain what the tool resolved and why.
- Keep the proof set runnable from a documented entrypoint so workspace and history behavior can be revalidated quickly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child tasks under this feature are completed.
- Workspace/history parity docs clearly distinguish implemented behavior from the remaining deltas now being closed.
- The project no longer needs a catch-all `hooks/workspace/history planned` parity row.
- The final scope is backed by focused code-level tests and shell e2e evidence with enough logging to diagnose discovery, ambiguity, and search-mode regressions.
notes:
Current state to preserve:
Explicit workspace config aggregation and robot/TUI history flows already exist. This feature exists because a smaller but real set of workspace/history gaps still remains.

title: Port workspace discovery and default-resolution behavior from legacy bv
description:
Background:
Rust supports explicit workspace config paths and namespacing, but not the full Go-style discovery and default behavior that made workspaces easier to use without fully explicit configuration on every command.

Goal:
Close the usability gap between explicit workspace support and the legacy default or discovery model.
design: Implementation expectations: Audit the legacy workspace type and config behavior and translate the user-facing semantics that matter. Implement discovery and default logic in a way that composes cleanly with the current loader and CLI path-resolution rules. Make tie-breaks, fallback order, and failure cases explicit so users can tell why a particular workspace was chosen or why none was. When discovery is ambiguous or fails, ensure the messaging includes concrete override guidance such as the relevant explicit flag or config path to use next. Document any intentional differences if the Rust architecture chooses a simpler but acceptable contract.
acceptance_criteria: Workspace commands can discover or default the same scenarios that the legacy Go tool supported, or documented deviations are explicit. Loader and CLI tests cover the new discovery and default semantics. When discovery is ambiguous or fails, the CLI explains what was searched, what candidates were found, and how to override the decision explicitly. Future agents can understand workspace behavior from local tests and bead notes alone.
notes:
Implemented workspace parity in two layers:
- CLI/source resolution now auto-discovers ancestor .bv/workspace.yaml when --workspace is omitted, for issue loading, watch-export path resolution, and background-mode reload config.
- Loader now understands legacy workspace discovery/default sections (discovery.enabled/patterns/exclude/max_depth and defaults.beads_path), auto-expands repo lists from common layouts, and emits explicit discovery-failure guidance.

Tests added/updated:
- src/loader.rs workspace discovery/default tests
- src/main.rs resolver/background/failure-message unit tests
- tests/conformance.rs end-to-end robot-triage test without explicit --workspace

Verification via rch:
- cargo test --lib workspace ✅
- cargo test --bin bvr resolve_issue_load_target_ ✅
- cargo test --bin bvr build_background_mode_config_discovers_workspace_without_flag ✅
- cargo test --bin bvr load_issues_reports_workspace_search_guidance_when_no_sources_exist ✅
- cargo test --test conformance workspace_robot_triage_auto_discovers_workspace_and_repo_sources ✅
- cargo check --all-targets ✅ (with unrelated pre-existing warnings)

title: Port history search modes and filters to TUI parity
description:
Background:
Rust history view already supports generic search and several navigation affordances, but the legacy Go history UI supported explicit search modes such as all, commit, SHA, bead, and author. The current parity docs had overclaimed this area.

Goal:
Add the missing history-search semantics so the history view supports the richer investigative workflows that existed in Go.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Port the actual search-mode behavior and operator affordances, not just extra filter strings.
- Integrate with the existing history keyflow and search model in a way that keeps the UI understandable.
- Make the active mode, mode-switch affordances, and zero-result states visible enough that users can tell what the search box currently means.
- Ensure the implementation stays testable through deterministic state and keyflow tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- History view supports the agreed legacy search modes or clearly documented equivalents.
- The UI exposes the active search mode and makes switching or interpreting modes understandable to users.
- Keyflow and unit coverage demonstrates switching modes, filtering, zero-result handling, and result navigation.
- `FEATURE_PARITY.md` no longer has to hedge or overclaim this behavior.
notes: This task touches `src/tui.rs`, but it is narrower than the broader board, insights, and graph parity work and therefore belongs in the workspace/history track.
close_reason: Implemented HistorySearchMode enum (All/Commit/Sha/Bead/Author) with Tab cycling during active search, mode-specific filtering in both Git and Bead views, mode label display in search status text, updated footer. 6 tests added, 4 snapshots updated.

title: Expand workspace and history unit, loader, and keyflow coverage
description:

Extend loader or conformance tests for workspace discovery and default behavior. Extend TUI keyflow or state tests for history search-mode behavior, zero-result handling, and result navigation. Keep the tests readable enough that future agents can recover the intended contract from them, and structure their outputs so the later shell-e2e bead can reuse the same mental model.
acceptance_criteria: Workspace and history parity behavior is covered by focused unit, loader or conformance, and keyflow tests rather than only by docs. The suite covers happy-path, representative edge-case, and negative scenarios, including ambiguous or missing workspaces and zero-result or mode-switching history searches. This bead is green before any shell-e2e workflow bead or final parity sweep claims the track is complete.

title: Add workspace/history e2e coverage with verbose discovery and navigation logging
description:
Background:
Workspace discovery and history search can look correct in focused tests while still confusing users in shell-driven workflows.

Goal:
Add workspace/history e2e coverage that exercises the real CLI or TUI entrypoints and logs discovery and navigation decisions in detail.
design: Implementation expectations: Create or extend a shell-level parity runner for workspace discovery/default behavior and history-mode workflows, preferably as a dedicated script such as `scripts/e2e_parity_workspace_history.sh` unless an existing harness is a better home. Exercise representative success cases plus at least one ambiguity or failure-oriented case for workspace resolution and one zero-result or mode-mismatch case for history search. Log resolved workspace paths, selection reasons, active history mode transitions, query strings, result counts, environment or fixture summaries, and retained temp repositories or debug dumps on failure. Run in strict shell mode and preserve enough context that a future agent can diagnose the failure from logs alone. Wire the script into the main e2e entrypoint or a documented parity-runner so workspace/history proof is easy to rerun consistently.
acceptance_criteria:
- The workspace/history e2e script proves both the main success path and representative failure or ambiguity paths for the track.
- It exits nonzero when discovery or history behavior diverges from the encoded contract.
- Failure logs isolate whether the regression came from workspace resolution, search-mode semantics, or result navigation, and they point to retained temp repositories or debug outputs.
- The script is reachable from a documented e2e entrypoint or parity-runner workflow.
- This evidence is required before docs or the final parity sweep can claim the workspace/history track is complete.
notes: This bead turns workspace and history parity into a shell-visible contract rather than a purely internal one.

title: Add workspace/history shell e2e script with discovery and search logging

Goal:

design:

acceptance_criteria:

- Logs make the resolution path and active history-mode behavior explicit enough to diagnose failures quickly.
- The script becomes part of the required verification evidence cited by the final sweep and final parity docs.
