title: Finish the remaining high-touch TUI parity surfaces
description:
Background:
Rust TUI parity is strong but still not complete. The audit confirmed that the partial rows for main detail richness, board, insights, graph, and full keybinding/interaction parity are real gaps rather than stale bookkeeping.

Goal:
Close the remaining TUI deltas so the interactive Rust experience can stand in for the legacy Go UI without caveats for the main investigative workflows.
design:
Execution constraint:
Most of this track touches `src/tui.rs`, snapshots, and keyflow tests. To reduce merge pain and partial-state regressions, these beads are intentionally serialized even when the technical dependencies are loose.

Approach:
- Land structural/detail-pane work first.
- Then progress through board, insights, and graph parity.
- Finish with expanded snapshots/keyflows that encode the final UX contract.
- Require the full verification ladder: deterministic snapshots or state or keyflow tests first, then end-to-end TUI journeys with retained failure artifacts and geometry or input logs.
- Keep the terminal proof runnable from a documented entrypoint so future agents can re-check the interactive contract without reconstructing the harness from scratch.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child tasks under this feature are completed.
- TUI parity docs can move the remaining partial rows with evidence instead of aspiration.
- Interactive workflows that were highlighted in the audit are represented in tests and snapshots.
- The final TUI scope is backed by deterministic code-level evidence plus end-to-end journeys whose logs and artifacts make regressions explainable.
notes: This track is intentionally separate from workspace/history because the main missing work here is broader UI richness and view-specific interaction parity, not loader behavior.

title: Enrich the main detail pane toward legacy Go parity
description:
Background:
The main Rust split view works, but the audit confirmed that the legacy Go main detail pane is still richer and more expressive, especially in how it renders issue detail context.

Goal:
Close the remaining detail-pane richness gap so the main view no longer feels like a simplified approximation.
design: Implementation expectations: Port the missing detail richness in a way that fits the Rust TUI architecture and existing layout model. Focus on the user-visible information architecture and detail rendering, not on mechanically copying the Go code. The result should remain readable for long titles, sparse metadata, and narrower terminal widths, and it should keep the currently selected issue or focus context obvious. Ensure any structural UI changes are reflected in snapshots or deterministic render tests.
acceptance_criteria: Main view detail content materially matches the intended legacy richness. The detail pane remains readable for long or sparse content and under representative narrow terminal widths, and it keeps selection or focus context obvious to the user. Snapshot or debug-render coverage exists for the updated main detail state, and the resulting implementation gives later board, insights, and graph work a stronger shared baseline.
notes: This bead is intentionally first in the TUI chain because it affects shared detail rendering expectations and can influence later view-specific work.

title: Port board-mode inline expansion and detail-scrolling parity
description:
Background:
Rust board mode is already functional, but the audit still found missing legacy behaviors such as inline expansion and richer detail scrolling or navigation semantics.

Goal:
Upgrade board mode from “usable” to legacy-equivalent for serious triage and planning workflows.
design: Implementation expectations: Port the board interactions that materially change how users inspect and manipulate lane content. Preserve the recent Rust board navigation work instead of replacing it wholesale. Ensure inline expansion, detail scrolling, and board navigation preserve orientation so users do not lose lane, selection, or expanded-state context. Extend keyflow and snapshot coverage to reflect the richer board-state model.
acceptance_criteria: Board mode supports the agreed inline and detail interactions needed for parity. Users can expand, inspect, and scroll board content without losing track of their current lane or selection context. Keyflow tests exercise the richer board interactions without relying on manual testing, and the board partial-parity row can be reevaluated from evidence after this bead.
notes: This bead is serialized after the main-detail bead because both heavily touch shared TUI detail rendering and selection behavior.

title: Port insights heatmap and drill-down interactions to parity
description:
Background:
Rust insights mode has solid core metrics and toggles, but the legacy Go UI still exposes richer exploratory affordances such as heatmap or drill-down style interactions.

Goal:
Make insights mode comparable to the legacy investigative workflow instead of merely presenting summary metrics.
design: Implementation expectations: Identify the specific interaction and visualization semantics that made the Go insights view more powerful. Port those capabilities in a Rust-native way that remains testable and coherent with the existing view-mode architecture. Keep explanation and calculation-proof affordances intact while expanding exploratory depth, and preserve clear context about what metric, slice, or drill-down target the user is currently inspecting.
acceptance_criteria: Insights mode supports the agreed parity interactions and richer drill-down behavior. Users can tell what they are drilling into, navigate back out without losing context, and understand the active metric or focus area. Snapshot or keyflow coverage demonstrates the new interactions, and the resulting view is meaningfully closer to the legacy power-user workflow.
notes: This bead follows board work for merge-safety reasons: both are high-churn edits in the same TUI module and test set.
close_reason: Added an insights heatmap submode with score-depth buckets, Enter/Esc drill behavior, list-pane navigation, detail-context sync, focused tests, and updated insights snapshots.

title: Port the dedicated visual graph pane to parity

Preserve the stronger current Rust metrics and deterministic rendering qualities while improving fidelity. Keep the result snapshot-friendly and robust under different terminal widths, sparse graphs, and focused-node exploration, and make focused node or edge context explicit enough that the user always knows what part of the graph they are inspecting.

It remains legible across representative terminal widths and sparse or dense graph states, and it keeps the currently focused node or edge context obvious. Snapshot, debug-render, or keyflow coverage demonstrates the richer graph experience, and the graph partial-parity row can be revisited from concrete evidence after this bead.

Use this bead as the code-level closure gate for all remaining TUI parity rows, and shape its outputs so the later end-to-end TUI journey bead can produce richer logs without redefining the contract.

The suite spans happy-path, representative edge-case, and failure-oriented render or interaction states, and failures clearly indicate which view, geometry, or interaction regressed. title: Add TUI parity end-to-end journeys with failure artifact capture
description:
Background:
The remaining TUI gaps are strongly interaction-driven, so snapshots alone are not enough to prove that the finished experience matches the intended legacy workflows.

Goal:
Add end-to-end TUI journeys with retained artifacts so regressions in main, board, insights, and graph workflows are easy to reproduce and understand.
design: Implementation expectations: Create or extend a deterministic TUI journey runner, such as a scripted keystroke harness, VHS capture, or another existing terminal-test tool the repo already uses, and use it to exercise the main-to-board-to-insights-to-graph investigative flows. Capture the key sequence, terminal dimensions, screen dumps or recordings, environment or fixture summaries, and the path to any failing snapshot or diff artifact. Include both a primary success journey and at least one failure-oriented or edge scenario such as narrow geometry, empty data, or a deliberately broken fixture that proves the state-dump diagnostics are useful. Make the output legible enough that a future agent can tell which view transition or interaction broke without re-instrumenting the harness. Wire the runner into the main e2e entrypoint or a documented parity-runner so interactive proof is as discoverable as the rest of the parity suite.
acceptance_criteria:
- The TUI e2e journeys cover the core interactive workflows closed by this parity push and include at least one representative edge or failure-oriented run with retained artifacts.
- Failures preserve enough information to show which keyflow, terminal geometry, render state, or fixture caused the regression.
- The journey runner is reachable from a documented e2e entrypoint or parity-runner workflow.
- This bead is required evidence before docs or the final parity sweep can claim the TUI track is complete.
notes: This bead is the heaviest TUI-proof layer and complements, rather than replaces, the faster snapshot and keyflow tests.

title: Add TUI parity end-to-end journeys with verbose state-dump logging

Snapshots and keyflows are necessary but not sufficient for the remaining TUI parity work. We also need an operator-level journey that records how the terminal experience behaves across the major parity surfaces and leaves usable diagnostics behind when it fails.

title: Align parity documentation and architecture promises with reality
description:
Background:
The audit showed that several parity docs were stale or overly optimistic, especially around hooks/workspace/history, history search modes, export/pages, and the claimed asupersync integration state.

Goal:
Make the documentation trustworthy so future work is driven by real implementation state instead of outdated claims or aspirational wording.
design:
Approach:
- Separate parity truth maintenance from implementation work, but block final closure on it.
- Treat `FEATURE_PARITY.md` as a living ledger backed by tests, not a marketing artifact.
- Clarify how asupersync fits into the architecture goal versus the narrower legacy-parity objective.
- Make the docs the discoverable control plane for verification: they should point to the unit, integration, and e2e entrypoints plus the logging and artifact conventions introduced by this parity push.
- Keep documentation updates trailing validated behavior so the written story always reflects the proven system rather than aspirational scope.
acceptance_criteria:
- Child tasks under this feature are completed.
- Parity and architecture docs match actual code/test state.
- Future agents can use the docs as trustworthy operational context instead of stale folklore.
- The docs tell a maintainer how to rerun the parity proof set and where to look when one of the parity scripts fails.
notes: This feature intentionally comes later in the graph because docs should trail validated behavior, not run ahead of it.

title: Decide and document the real asupersync scope relative to legacy parity
description:
Background:
The audit found that current docs describe asupersync integration more generously than the code supports. The project still has an architectural aspiration here, but it is not the same thing as current parity with the legacy Go tool.

Goal:
Write down a crisp decision about whether asupersync is a parity-adjacent future enhancement, an active in-flight integration, or a deliberately deferred architectural path.
design:
Implementation expectations:
- Inspect current feature flags, runtime usage, and architectural docs.
- Choose wording that distinguishes “legacy parity” from “future Rust-native architecture goals.”
- Avoid overstating implemented integration while preserving long-term design intent if it still stands.
acceptance_criteria:
- `README.md`, `FEATURE_PARITY.md`, and related architecture docs describe asupersync honestly.
- Future agents can tell whether asupersync work is in scope for parity or belongs to a separate post-parity roadmap.
- No stale wording remains that implies runtime integration already exists when it does not.
notes: This bead is mostly documentation and scope control, but it matters because stale architecture promises distort planning priorities and parity status.

title: Finalize README and FEATURE_PARITY.md after the remaining parity work lands
description:
Background:
The project now has a large enough parity surface that stale docs actively create planning errors. Final cleanup must happen after the behavior and tests are actually in place.

Goal:
Produce a final parity ledger and README state that a future agent can trust without rereading historic plans or session transcripts.
design: Implementation expectations: Update parity rows, known risks, verification sections, and any README capability claims that drifted during implementation. Cite actual tests, shell e2e scripts, and verification entrypoints rather than vague done statements. Add a compact verification cookbook so a future agent or maintainer can see which `rch` commands and parity e2e scripts demonstrate the completed scope, how those scripts are invoked from the main e2e entrypoint or parity-runner workflow, and where they retain logs or artifacts on failure. Ensure any remaining partials or planned work, if any, are explicit and justified.
acceptance_criteria:
- `FEATURE_PARITY.md` and `README.md` accurately reflect implemented state and remaining gaps.
- Stale claims found in the audit are removed or corrected.
- The docs are self-consistent, aligned with the test and e2e evidence available at the end of the parity push, and explicit about how to rerun the proof set, including the unit, integration, and parity-script entrypoints plus the retained log or artifact locations that matter.
notes: This bead should be one of the last pieces of work because its value depends on the implementation, automated tests, and shell e2e evidence being complete first.

title: Run the final legacy parity verification sweep and close the umbrella epic
description:
Background:
The project has accumulated enough parity work that a final “everything important still agrees” sweep is required before the umbrella epic can be closed.

Goal:
Perform the closing verification pass for the remaining parity push and make an explicit go or no-go decision on whether `bvr` can now claim full legacy replacement status.
design: Implementation expectations: Run the final required `rch` validation commands, the track-specific lib or integration suites, and the parity e2e scripts that were added by this plan. Use the same documented entrypoint structure promised by the docs beads, and verify that those instructions actually work. Record the exact command matrix, note where logs or artifacts live, and summarize any residual gaps honestly if total parity still has exceptions. Close `bd-7oo` only when implementation, tests, shell e2e evidence, and docs agree.
acceptance_criteria:
- Required `rch` validation passes for the completed parity scope.
- The final bead summary records what is truly complete versus what, if anything, remains deferred, and it references the concrete verification commands, the parity e2e entrypoints that were exercised, and any retained log or artifact paths that matter.
- The final sweep confirms that the documented verification cookbook is accurate.
- `bd-7oo` is only closed if the remaining child graph and documentation justify that claim.
notes: This bead is the explicit anti-handwave mechanism. It now covers the full verification ladder, including shell e2e evidence and diagnostic artifacts, so the project does not silently drift from almost there to claimed complete.

title: Analysis engine unit tests + conformance + e2e
description: WHAT: Comprehensive test coverage for all analysis engine additions. THREE layers: (1) Unit tests per module — ImpactScore computation with known graph fixtures (verify each of 8 components), TriageScoringOptions weight application, StaleCleanup detection accuracy, WhatIf delta correctness, advanced insights (TopKSet, CoverageSet, KPaths, CycleBreak) against hand-calculated expected results. (2) Conformance tests: generate Go reference fixtures for triage scoring on shared test graphs, compare Rust output. (3) E2e script (scripts/test_analysis_parity.sh) that builds test .beads/ directories with known issue graphs, runs both bv --robot-triage and bvr --robot-triage, compares scoring order and component breakdown. LOGGING: Every test should log the input graph structure, expected scores, actual scores, and delta. On failure, dump the full graph and all intermediate metric values. FIXTURES: Create at least 3 test graphs — (a) simple linear chain (5 nodes), (b) complex with cycles and clusters (20+ nodes), (c) edge case with single node and empty graph. ACCEPTANCE: All analysis modules have >90% line coverage. Conformance fixtures match. E2e script passes. PARENT: bd-ybj
close_reason: Added 75 new analysis unit tests: suggest.rs (22 new: duplicates, missing deps, labels, cycles, helpers), forecast.rs (20 new: median, complexity, velocity, confidence, agents), alerts.rs (19 new: stale thresholds, in_progress multiplier, cascade severity, filters, summarize), label_intel.rs (18 new: velocity, freshness, flow, criticality, composite health). Fixed 2 snapshot tests. Total lib tests: 619.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: TESTING STRATEGY: Analysis tests are the most important because scoring bugs are silent — wrong scores don't crash, they just produce bad triage recommendations. Every test should use deterministic graph fixtures (no randomness). Use property-based testing where possible (e.g., all ImpactScore components sum to total, WhatIf deltas are symmetric). The 3-graph fixture set (linear, complex, edge-case) should be shared across all analysis tests. Go conformance may not be exact due to floating-point — use epsilon comparison (1e-6).

title: Analysis engine depth: scoring transparency, WhatIf, advanced insights
description: The Go analysis engine (pkg/analysis/) has several sophisticated modules that provide deeper analysis than Rust currently offers. While the core algorithms (PageRank, betweenness, HITS, eigenvector, k-core, cycles) are fully ported, the INTERPRETATION layers on top are simplified or missing. This epic ports the remaining analysis depth: score breakdowns for transparency, configurable triage weights, WhatIf scenario analysis, and advanced graph optimization insights. These modules power the richer robot output fields (top_what_ifs, advanced_insights, feedback) and give AI agents the detailed reasoning they need to make good decisions. Go analysis source: legacy_beads_viewer_code/beads_viewer/pkg/analysis/ (priority.go, whatif.go, advanced_insights.go, config.go). Rust analysis: src/analysis/ (triage.rs, plan.rs, suggest.rs, graph.rs).
close_reason: All sub-beads complete: bd-ybj.1 (ImpactScore 8-component), bd-ybj.2 (TriageScoringOptions), bd-ybj.4 (WhatIf), bd-ybj.5 (AdvancedInsights 6 algorithms), bd-6gv (AnalysisConfig). Analysis engine now has full scoring transparency, configurable triage weights, what-if scenario planning, and 6 advanced graph algorithms.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CONTEXT: Go's analysis engine has richer scoring (8-component ImpactScore with ScoreBreakdown), configurable triage weights, WhatIf scenario analysis, and advanced graph insights. Our Rust engine computes correct metrics but uses a simplified 4-factor triage formula and lacks scenario planning. This epic deepens the analysis to match. ORDER: bd-ybj.1 (ImpactScore) is foundational — everything else builds on it. bd-ybj.2 (TriageScoringOptions) next. bd-ybj.3-5 can parallelize after that.

title: Implement ImpactScore with 8-component ScoreBreakdown for triage transparency
description: Go's priority.go defines ImpactScore with a detailed ScoreBreakdown showing exactly how each issue's score is composed: PageRank (weight 0.22), Betweenness (0.20), BlockerRatio (0.13), Staleness (0.05), PriorityBoost (0.10), TimeToImpact (0.10), Urgency (0.10), Risk (0.10). Each component is normalized 0-1 and the breakdown is included in robot outputs so agents can understand WHY an issue ranks high. Rust's triage.rs uses a simplified 4-factor formula (0.45*pagerank + 0.30*unblocks + 0.20*priority + 0.05*urgency) without component-level breakdown. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Create ImpactScore struct in src/analysis/triage.rs with fields: issue_id, title, score, breakdown. (2) ScoreBreakdown: 8 components each with weight/raw/weighted. (3) Implement compute_impact_scores() that calculates each component separately. (4) Expose breakdown in Recommendation struct for robot outputs. FILES: src/analysis/triage.rs, src/main.rs robot output structs. Go ref: priority.go ScoreBreakdown struct and ComputeImpactScores(). ACCEPTANCE: robot-triage recommendations include breakdown object showing all 8 components.
close_reason: Implemented 8-component ImpactScore (PageRank/0.22, Betweenness/0.20, BlockerRatio/0.13, Staleness/0.05, PriorityBoost/0.10, TimeToImpact/0.10, Urgency/0.10, Risk/0.10) with ScoreBreakdown in triage.rs. Breakdown included in Recommendation struct. 8 new unit tests including 100-node graph. All 661 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/analysis/triage.go ImpactScore struct — 8 components: GraphCentrality, BlockingFactor, ClusterImportance, RecencyWeight, LabelHealth, UnblockPotential, QuickWinScore, AttentionScore. Each gets a ScoreBreakdown{Raw, Normalized, Weight, WeightedContribution, Explanation}. Currently Rust uses 4 factors with opaque float scores. The breakdown is what makes triage EXPLAINABLE — users can see WHY an issue ranks where it does.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CORRECTION: Previous description used invented field names (GraphCentrality, ClusterImportance, etc). The ACTUAL Go ImpactScore components from priority.go lines 24-51 are: PageRank (weight 0.22), Betweenness (weight 0.20), BlockerRatio (weight 0.13), Staleness (weight 0.05), PriorityBoost (weight 0.10), TimeToImpact (weight 0.10), Urgency (weight 0.10), Risk (weight 0.10). Each component has a weighted value field (e.g., PageRank), a normalized pre-weight field (e.g., PageRankNorm), and some have explanation strings (TimeToImpactExplanation, UrgencyExplanation, RiskExplanation). Risk also has a nested RiskSignals struct. Implementation must match these EXACT field names and weights.

title: Implement TriageScoringOptions for configurable triage weights and thresholds
description: Go's TriageScoringOptions: BaseScoreWeight (0.70), UnblockBoostWeight (0.15), QuickWinWeight (0.15), UnblockThreshold (5), QuickWinMaxDepth (2), plus EnableLabelHealth/EnableClaimPenalty/EnableAttentionScore flags. Rust has hardcoded weights. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Define TriageScoringOptions struct in src/analysis/triage.rs. (2) DefaultTriageScoringOptions() with Go defaults. (3) Modify compute_triage() to accept options. (4) Add unblock_boost and quick_win_boost calculations. (5) Final score = base_weight*impact + unblock_weight*boost + quickwin_weight*bonus. Go ref: triage.go. ACCEPTANCE: Triage uses configurable weights; defaults match Go.
close_reason: Implemented TriageScoringOptions with configurable base_score_weight (0.70), unblock_boost_weight (0.15), quick_win_weight (0.15), thresholds (unblock=5, quick_win_max_depth=2), and 3 feature flags (enable_label_health, enable_claim_penalty, enable_attention_score — all false by default). compute_triage() now uses 3-level composite scoring: base*impact + unblock*boost + quickwin*bonus. 6 unit tests. 685 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/analysis/triage.go DefaultTriageScoringOptions() — BaseScoreWeight=0.70, UnblockBoostWeight=0.15, QuickWinWeight=0.15, plus per-component weight overrides. Users can tune weights in config. Currently Rust hardcodes weights. DEPENDS ON bd-ybj.1 because the options reference ImpactScore components.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: ADDITIONAL DETAIL: Go's TriageScoringOptions (triage.go:1012-1027) has 3 weight fields + 2 threshold fields + 3 feature flags + 1 agent identity field. Feature flags: EnableLabelHealth (Phase 2), EnableClaimPenalty (Phase 3), EnableAttentionScore (Phase 4) — all false by default (MVP mode). ClaimedByAgent tracks which agent is computing (for multi-agent claim penalty). This phased rollout design is elegant — implement all flags but default to off, matching Go's conservative MVP approach. The Rust implementation should also include UnblockThreshold (default 5) and QuickWinMaxDepth (default 2).

title: Implement WhatIf analysis module for scenario planning
description: Go's whatif.go computes 'what if we completed this issue' deltas: direct_unblocks (issues immediately unblocked), transitive_unblocks (total downstream unblocks), estimated_days_saved, pagerank_delta (how graph centrality shifts), metric_changes. This powers the top_what_ifs robot output and helps agents identify highest-leverage items. IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Create src/analysis/whatif.rs module. (2) WhatIfDelta struct: issue_id, direct_unblocks (Vec<String>), transitive_unblocks (Vec<String>), estimated_days_saved (f64), pagerank_delta (f64). (3) compute_what_if(graph, issue_id) -> WhatIfDelta: simulate removing the issue from the graph, recompute metrics on the modified graph, diff results. (4) top_what_if_deltas(graph, issues, top_n) -> Vec<WhatIfDelta>: compute for all open issues, sort by transitive_unblocks desc. (5) Wire into Analyzer. Go ref: whatif.go WhatIfDelta, ComputeWhatIfDelta(), TopWhatIfDeltas(). ACCEPTANCE: Analyzer.what_if(issue_id) returns meaningful deltas. Top-5 what-ifs match Go's ranking for reference fixtures.
close_reason: Implemented WhatIf analysis module (src/analysis/whatif.rs) with WhatIfDelta struct (direct_unblocks, transitive_unblocks, estimated_days_saved, pagerank_delta, cycles_broken). compute_what_if() simulates issue completion by rebuilding graph and diffing metrics. top_what_if_deltas() ranks all open issues by impact. Wired into Analyzer with what_if() and top_what_ifs() methods. 11 unit tests including chain, cycle, empty, single-node, large-graph (100 nodes). 696 tests pass.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/analysis/whatif.go — WhatIf takes a proposed action (close issue X, add dependency X→Y, remove label Z) and returns the delta in all graph metrics. Implementation: clone the graph, apply the mutation, recompute metrics, diff against baseline. Cache (bd-4h6) makes this fast by avoiding full recomputation when only one node changes.

title: Implement advanced insights: TopKSet, CoverageSet, KPaths, CycleBreak, ParallelCut, ParallelGain
description: Go's advanced_insights.go (~700 lines) provides sophisticated graph optimization algorithms: (1) TopKSet: K items that maximize downstream unlocks using greedy submodular optimization. (2) CoverageSet: minimal set of items covering all critical paths (vertex cover approximation). (3) KPaths: K-shortest critical paths through the dependency graph (Yen's algorithm). (4) ParallelCut: edges to remove for maximum parallelization (min-cut variant). (5) CycleBreak: suggested edges to break cycles with minimal collateral damage (from Go's bv-graph-wasm cycle_break_suggestions). IMPLEMENTATION: (1) Create src/analysis/advanced.rs. (2) TopKSet: greedy set-cover algorithm over transitive_unblocks. (3) CoverageSet: greedy vertex cover on critical path DAG. (4) KPaths: BFS/DFS-based K-shortest paths using the critical_path infrastructure. (5) CycleBreak: port from Go's cycle_break_suggestions (already in bv-graph-wasm/cycles.rs). (6) AdvancedInsights struct aggregating all results. (7) Wire into Analyzer.advanced_insights(). Go ref: advanced_insights.go. ACCEPTANCE: Unit tests for each algorithm. Results appear in --robot-insights when computed.
close_reason: Implemented 6 advanced insight algorithms (TopKSet, CoverageSet, KPaths, CycleBreak, ParallelCut, ParallelGain) in src/analysis/advanced.rs with 15 unit tests, all passing

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: Go reference: pkg/analysis/advanced.go — Four advanced analysis types: (1) TopKSet: nodes appearing in top-K across multiple metrics simultaneously (the 'keystone' issues). (2) CoverageSet: greedy minimum set that touches all clusters (for maximum impact work). (3) KPaths: k-shortest paths between specified nodes using Yen's algorithm. (4) CycleBreak: minimum feedback arc set to break all cycles (NP-hard, use greedy approximation). All four use petgraph algorithms.

comments.author: Dicklesworthstone
comments.text: CORRECTION: Previous description listed only 4 advanced insight types but Go has 6. The full AdvancedInsights struct from advanced_insights.go lines 58-84 contains: TopKSet (submodular selection for max unlock), CoverageSet (greedy vertex cover), KPaths (k-shortest critical paths), CycleBreak (minimum feedback arc set), PLUS two additional types: ParallelCut (parallel cut analysis) and ParallelGain (parallel gain analysis). The Rust implementation must include all 6 types. The AdvancedInsightsConfig and UsageHints fields must also be included.
